significance: Politically charged follow-up to 'In My Tribe'; tackled apartheid and homelessness.
Breakthrough alt-rock album; includes cover of Cat Stevens' 'Peace Train' (later pulled from pressings).
UK acid house/techno pioneers' fourth album, blending rave energy with pop hooks.

album: Headlines and Deadlines: The Hits of a-ha
significance: Compiles the synth-pop trio's biggest hits including 'Take On Me.'
Career-spanning hits set from the sophisti-pop/new wave group behind 'The Lexicon of Love.'
Debut album, one of best-selling debuts ever; 'The Sign' became a global smash.
Third album, more polished pop-dance sound, less commercially dominant than debut.
Debut album named for her age at the time; launched her career with 'Chasing Pavements.'
Fourth studio album returning to a warmer, more organic sound after 'Pocket Symphony.'
Landmark French downtempo/electronic debut; defined the 'chillout' genre of the late '90s.
More electronic-leaning album produced with Guy Sigsworth after her divorce from Ryan Reynolds.
One of the best-selling albums ever; raw confessional rock that defined mid-90s alt-pop.
Solo debut from the UB40 frontman after decades fronting the reggae band.
Compiles hits from her solo career after Yazoo, including 'All Cried Out' and 'Only You.'
Their most commercially successful album, blending goth-folk with radio-friendly melodies.
Fourth album; softer, more acoustic direction than their earlier goth-rock sound.
Comeback album after a hiatus, later reissue expanded the band's later catalogue.
Compilation of the British girl group's biggest singles, including 'Never Ever' and 'Pure Shores.'
Remix/hits compilation reworking the German synth-pop band's classics for the 2000s dance scene.
Comeback album after years away; retained the band's signature lush synth sound.
Follow-up to their breakout debut, includes 'Sounds Like a Melody.'
Swiss harpist's ambient/new age work, part of his signature electro-acoustic harp style.
His debut album, pioneering electronically processed harp compositions.
Features guest vocalists including Carly Simon; continues his new age harp signature.
Compiles the big beat/electronic act's remixes and singles, including 'Stop the Rock.'
Annual mix compilation from the trance DJ's globally influential ASOT radio series.

album: The Best of the Art of Noise
significance: Compiles work from the pioneering sampling/production collective behind 'Close (to the Edit).'
Solo debut after The Verve's split; UK #1 album, includes 'A Song for the Lovers.'
Second solo album, more orchestral and introspective than his debut.
UK reggae band's alternative mixes album, reworking hits with guest MCs.
Vocal trance/pop project associated with the trance compilation scene of the late '90s.
Her commercial peak solo album, includes 'Leave a Light On' and 'Summer Rain.'
Hits compilation covering her post-Go-Go's solo pop career.
Compiles the new wave band's hits including 'Take My Breath Away' from Top Gun.
Debut solo album after leaving Generation X; includes 'White Wedding' and 'Dancing with Myself.'
Comeback album after a near-fatal motorcycle accident; includes 'Cradle of Love.'
Concept album exploring early internet/cyberpunk themes; commercially unsuccessful but ambitious.
His commercial and critical peak; title track and 'Eyes Without a Face' are signature hits.
Remix compilation reworking his hits for club play.
Includes 'To Be a Lover'; continued his mid-80s hard rock/pop-punk crossover.
Career-spanning hits from one of rock's most significant songwriters and performers.
Combines solo Bryan Ferry and Roxy Music hits into one collection.
Trance/electronic producer's influential sophomore album, pushing progressive trance forward.
UK reggae-pop artist's album featuring the hit 'Sweets for My Sweet.'
Swedish band's breakthrough album featuring the global hit 'Lovefool.'
Cellist/vocalist known for her Celtic-crossover style, having worked with Peter Gabriel and Massive Attack.
The band's final studio album before their 1988 breakup.
Compiles the new wave band's biggest singles including 'Just What I Needed.'
Includes 'The Power of Love'; marked her transition to English-language pop superstardom.
Third album, more personal and introspective than her celebrated debut.
Multi-platinum comeback album, includes the Grammy-winning hit 'Give Me One Reason.'
Hits compilation from the underrated English new wave/sophisti-pop band.
Second studio album, refining his rockabilly-noir sound before mainstream breakthrough.
Deeply personal breakup album, considered one of his best; includes 'Somebody's Crying.'
Includes 'Wicked Game,' famously featured in David Lynch's 'Wild at Heart.'
Follow-up to his breakthrough, continued his rockabilly-noir sound.
Reissue/compilation capitalizing on the massive success of the title single.
Compiles decades of hits from the British singer-songwriter known for 'Road to Hell.'
Their major-label debut for Capitol; ethereal dream pop with denser production.
More accessible, clearer vocals than earlier work; often seen as a turning point.
Debut album; raw, gothic-tinged post-punk foundation of their dream pop sound.
Widely considered their masterpiece; lush, melodic dream pop landmark.

album: A Rush of Blood to the Head
significance: Massively acclaimed second album; includes 'Clocks' and 'The Scientist.'
Concept album with a more electronic, arena-pop direction; includes 'Paradise.'
Debut album that launched the band; includes 'Yellow.'

album: Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends
significance: Grammy Album of the Year; produced by Brian Eno, marked a sonic reinvention.
Their biggest-selling studio album; includes 'Speed of Sound' and 'Fix You.'
Debut album blending Irish traditional music with pop; hugely successful in Europe.
Fourth album, includes 'Promises'; continued their alt-rock success post-'Zombie.'

album: Everybody Else Is Doing It, So Why Can't We?
significance: Debut album with 'Linger' and 'Dreams'; defined 90s Irish alt-rock.

album: The Very Best of Crowded House
significance: Hits compilation including 'Don't Dream It's Over' and 'Weather with You.'

album: This Time: The First Four Years
significance: Compiles the band's hits from their imperial early-80s New Romantic period.
Debut album featuring the transatlantic #1 hit '(I Just) Died in Your Arms.'

album: Twelve Deadly Cyns... and Then Some
significance: Hits compilation spanning her 80s pop peak with 'Girls Just Want to Have Fun.'

album: Shaken and Stirred: The David Arnold James Bond Project
significance: Reimagines classic Bond themes with alt/electronic artists; Arnold later scored several Bond films himself.
Grammy-winning album blending Eastern European folk vocals with ambient/world electronica.
Hits collection from the arena rock band behind 'Hysteria' and 'Pyromania.'
Early singles compilation, includes 'Just Can't Get Enough' and 'People Are People.'
Third studio album, more electronic-influenced than her earlier work.
Her biggest-selling album; includes 'White Flag.'
More stripped-down, atmospheric album than her earlier pop-leaning work.
One of the first albums mixed for CD; includes 'Money for Nothing' and 'Walk of Life.'
Hits compilation named after their MTV-era anthem.
Their final studio album before the band's dissolution.
Debut album featuring the global hit 'I Love You Always Forever.'
Blends dub, breakbeat and folk influences; a cult favourite of UK electronic music.
Debut album; indie-electronic with a distinctly British melancholic pop sound.
Sophomore album continuing their synth-pop/indie sound.
Live reunion album, ending the band's famous 14-year hiatus; includes new studio tracks.
Solo album from the former Fairground Attraction singer, includes 'Town Without Pity.'
Debut from the American progressive rock band, drawing Rush/Yes influences.

album: Le Roi Est Mort, Vive Le Roi!
significance: Fourth album continuing Enigma's Gregorian-chant-meets-electronica formula.
Debut album; 'Sadeness (Part I)' pioneered the ambient/Gregorian chant dance genre.
Includes 'Return to Innocence,' one of the era's most recognisable ambient hits.

album: The Fall of a Rebel Angel
significance: Later-career release continuing the project's atmospheric, spiritually-themed electronica.
Includes tracks in the constructed Loxian language; continued her signature Celtic-ambient sound.
Later career album, her first in seven years at time of release.
Her commercial breakthrough in the US; Grammy Award-winning.
Soundtrack originally composed for a BBC documentary series; her debut solo release.
Grammy-winning album continuing her ambient/Celtic style.
Career-spanning hits compilation.
Global breakthrough album; includes 'Orinoco Flow,' a worldwide #1 hit.
Includes the title track, a US #1 hit; marked Estefan's crossover to English-language pop.
Compiles the duo's biggest hits including 'Sweet Dreams (Are Made of This).'
Contains 'Missing,' later remixed into a massive dance hit by Todd Terry.
Debut album, jazz-inflected sophisti-pop that established the duo's sound.
Marked their full transition into drum & bass/electronic territory.
Includes 'We Come 1'; continued their run of anthemic electronic singles.
Debut album, includes 'Insomnia,' a defining 90s dance anthem.
Later album continuing the group's spiritually-tinged electronic sound.
Electroclash act's second album, more polished pop-production than their cult debut.
Rare lineup album without Lindsey Buckingham or Stevie Nicks; commercially unsuccessful.
Hits compilation from the arena rock band behind 'I Want to Know What Love Is.'
Smooth jazz supergroup album featuring Bob James, Lee Ritenour, Nathan East, Harvey Mason.

album: Shaking the Tree: Sixteen Golden Greats
significance: Hits compilation spanning his art-rock solo career, includes 'Sledgehammer.'
Grammy-nominated sophomore album; includes 'Push It' and 'I Think I'm Paranoid.'
Career retrospective marking 25 years since Wham!'s debut.
Compiles the flamenco-pop group's biggest hits including 'Bamboleo.'
Marked a shift to glam-electro sound after the ambient 'Felt Mountain.'
Debut album, cinematic trip-hop/ambient style, Mercury Prize nominated.
Full embrace of 80s synth-pop sound, a deliberate stylistic pivot.
Includes 'Ooh La La'; glam-disco direction, one of their most commercially successful records.
New age/world guitarist known for blending Native American and flamenco influences.
Hits compilation featuring 'Slave to the Rhythm' and 'Pull Up to the Bumper'; iconic Jean-Paul Goude cover art.
Later-period album from the British dance/downtempo duo.
Compiles synth-pop essentials including 'Don't You Want Me.'
Comeback album after years away; includes 'Tell Me When.'
Budget reissue of the Spanish superstar's crossover pop catalogue.
Album released after the band's commercial peak, preceding Michael Hutchence's death in 1997.
His breakthrough mainstream album; includes 'Better Together' and 'Banana Pancakes.'
German trance duo's influential concept album blending ambient and dance.
Includes 'Virtual Insanity'; one of the best-selling UK albums of the 90s.
Compilation of the influential art-rock/new wave band fronted by David Sylvian.
Double compilation of unreleased material and live recordings from the guitar virtuoso.
Experimented with electronic/dance production, atypical for the heartland rocker.
Later-period album showcasing her continued lyrical and musical sophistication.
From the DJ credited with founding the Café del Mar chillout sound.
Sophisti-pop/jazz-pop singer-songwriter known for a rich vocal style.
Career-defining album; includes 'Constant Craving,' won a Grammy.
Definitive hits compilation, includes a re-recorded 'Wuthering Heights' vocal.
Debut album, UK's best-selling album of 2004; includes 'Somewhere Only We Know.'
Marked a more synth-driven sound compared to their piano-rock beginnings.
Darker, more layered second album; includes 'Is It Any Wonder?'
Best-selling instrumental album of all time; smooth jazz landmark.
Only studio album from the influential/controversial acid house/pop act.
First solo album after Dire Straits, featuring Celtic and country textures.
Compiles film score work including Local Hero and The Princess Bride.
Marked her reinvention with Stock Aitken Waterman-era pop sound continuing into dance territory.

album: Born to Die: The Paradise Edition
significance: Expanded reissue of her breakout debut, adding the 'Paradise' EP.
Compiles the pioneering British progressive house/electronic act's key tracks.
Genre-defining debut album, foundational to British progressive house/big beat.
Includes 'The Life of Riley'; core to the Britpop-adjacent jangle-pop era.
Debut album that made her a UK pop breakthrough; includes 'Smile.'
Second album, includes 'The Fear'; UK #1 album.
Third album referencing Kanye West's 'Yeezus'; more explicit pop/production.
Sophomore album continuing her blue-eyed soul sound after 'Affection.'
Third album, includes 'In All the Right Places.'
Critically acclaimed debut; atmospheric indie-pop with Hannah Reid's distinctive vocals.
Mercury Prize-winning album; includes 'Moving On Up.'
Expanded/remixed edition following the Mercury Prize win.
Later album continuing their dance-soul sound with a slicker production style.
Solo album from the Clannad singer, blending Celtic and pop textures.
New age/instrumental concept album inspired by Greek mythology.
New age/instrumental concept album inspired by Greek mythology.
French house/dance producer's album featuring international collaborations.
Likely their self-titled logo-cover release, part of the trip-hop pioneers' catalogue.
Long-awaited comeback album after a seven-year gap; darker, more collaborative.
Second album, deepened the trip-hop sound they helped pioneer on 'Blue Lines.'
Landmark neo-soul debut; helped define the genre alongside D'Angelo.

album: Beggar on a Beach of Gold
significance: Album from the Genesis-adjacent supergroup led by Mike Rutherford.
Compiles the band's biggest singles including 'The Living Years.'
Later reunion-era album spotlighting vocalist Paul Carrack.
Concept album inspired by Arthur C. Clarke's novel of the same name.
Explores Celtic musical themes across an instrumental suite.
Concept album structured as a night out, from electronic dance to comedown.
Landmark album blending old blues/gospel samples with electronica; every track was licensed for media.
Later album continuing the trip-hop/downtempo sound of the Godfrey siblings' project.
Hits compilation including 'The Sea' and 'Rome Wasn't Built in a Day.'
Debut album, established their smoky trip-hop sound alongside Massive Attack and Portishead.
Includes two US #1 hits: 'Broken Wings' and 'Kyrie.'

Blends Arabic music with Western electronic/pop production.

Career-defining reinvention with Timbaland; includes 'Promiscuous' and 'Say It Right.'

Singles compilation, essential document of their post-Joy Division synth-rock evolution.
Later album from Danny Elfman's band before he shifted fully to film scoring.
Post-reunion-era album under Andy McCluskey's sole direction of the OMD name.
Comeback hit album, includes 'Sailing on the Seven Seas' and 'Pandora's Box.'
Compiles the synth-pop pioneers' hits including 'Enola Gay.'
Later studio album before the band's extended hiatus.

album: The Orb's Adventures Beyond the Ultraworld
significance: Landmark ambient house debut; helped define the genre.
UK #1 album; includes 'Blue Room,' one of the longest singles to chart in the UK.
Compiles work from the producer later known for Madonna's 'Ray of Light.'
Includes 'Stupid Girls' and 'U + Ur Hand'; marked her more rock-oriented pop sound.
Compiles solo hits from the former Jam/Style Council frontman's Britpop-era comeback.

album: Shaking the Tree: Sixteen Golden Greats
significance: Hits compilation spanning his art-rock solo career.
Includes 'Another Day in Paradise'; his most political and socially conscious album.
Landmark rock opera/concept album; one of the best-selling albums in history.
Compiles the trio's biggest singles including 'Every Breath You Take.'
Long-awaited second album, darker and more abrasive than 'Dummy.'
Mercury Prize-winning debut; foundational trip-hop album alongside Massive Attack.
Live acoustic album reworking the band's catalogue with orchestral arrangements.
Compilation from the ZTT-signed synth act, best known for 'Duel.'
Cult classic art-pop/synth album, highly influential despite modest chart success.
Big beat landmark; includes 'History Repeating' with Shirley Bassey.
Mercury Prize-nominated album that set up Pulp's imperial Britpop period.
Best-selling album in UK chart history; spans their most iconic singles.
Final studio album, completed after Freddie Mercury's death using his last vocal recordings.
Hits compilation from the jazz-soul vocalist behind 'One Day I'll Fly Away.'
Fourth studio album, continuing his adult-contemporary pop-rock sound.
Marked her transformation into an edgier pop icon; includes 'Umbrella.'
Follow-up to 'Dreamland,' continuing his ambient/dream trance style.
Includes 'Children,' a genre-defining dream trance anthem.
House music producer's album featuring 'Another Chance,' a UK #1 single.
Continued the Swedish duo's run of melodic pop-rock hits.
Rarities/live/new tracks album released amid their global touring peak.
Their final and most polished studio album; 'More Than This' is a defining art-pop track.
Debut album; 'Smooth Operator' helped define sophisti-pop/soul of the 80s.

German Eurodance/pop singer's mid-90s studio album.

Studio album from the 80s/90s Eurodance star behind 'Maria Magdalena.'
Compiles decades of Latin rock hits from the guitar legend.

Glam-punk/electronic act known for selling advertising space between album tracks.
